title: Physics-Informed Residual Deep Learning for Constitutive Modeling of Hot Deformation and Dynamic Recrystallization in a Mo-Rich $α+β$ Titanium Alloy
authors: Prashil S. Joshi, Diksha Mahadule, Rajesh K. Khatirkar
abstract: Accurate constitutive modeling of hot deformation behavior is essential for designing thermomechanical processes in advanced structural alloys. Conventional Arrhenius-type and empirical models do not adequately capture the combined effects of strain hardening, dynamic recovery (DRV), and dynamic recrystallization (DRX) across broad processing conditions. In this study, two Stacked Residual Physics-Informed Neural Networks (STAR-PINNs) were developed to simulate the hot deformation response of a Mo-rich $α+β$ titanium alloy (Ti-6Al-4Mo-1V-0.1Si). The Enhanced STAR-PINN incorporated thermomechanical constitutive constraints, while the DRX-Aware STAR-PINN employed a dual-output architecture to account for recrystallization kinetics. Both models used a shared residual encoder trained on experimental flow stress data collected at temperatures from 800 to 1050 degrees C and strain rates between 0.01 and 10 per second. Physics-informed constraints, including thermal softening, strain-rate sensitivity, strain hardening, and post-peak softening, were enforced through automatic differentiation. The DRX-Aware model further integrated JMAK-Avrami regularization, DRX saturation constraints, and Arrhenius-based consistency with tunable parameters, directly linking the predicted DRX fraction to stress output via latent-feature fusion. The DRX-Aware STAR-PINN achieved RMSE = 11.69 MPa, MAE = 4.83 MPa, R^2 = 0.9850, and a cross-validated RMSE of 12.47 +/- 0.26 MPa. This model accurately reproduced temperature-dependent flow curves, DRX kinetics, and Zener-Hollomon relationships, while maintaining physically consistent constitutive behavior. These results demonstrate that physics-informed deep learning provides a robust and interpretable framework for constitutive modeling, offering a practical approach for advanced process modeling of titanium alloys.

infographic.key_insight: A physics-informed residual deep learning model can predict hot deformation flow stress and dynamic recrystallization in a Mo-rich b1+b2 titanium alloy with high accuracy while preserving constitutive physics.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This enables more reliable thermomechanical process design for titanium alloys by capturing strain hardening, softening, and recrystallization effects across broad temperature and strain-rate conditions.
method: Stacked Residual Physics-Informed Neural Networks (STAR-PINNs) trained on flow stress data from 800�b71050 �b0C and 0.01�b710 s�b9 strain rates
Enhanced STAR-PINN enforced thermomechanical constitutive constraints via automatic differentiation
DRX-Aware STAR-PINN added dual outputs, JMAK-Avrami regularization, DRX saturation constraints, and Arrhenius consistency
infographic.evidence_signal: The DRX-Aware model achieved RMSE = 11.69 MPa, MAE = 4.83 MPa, R�b2 = 0.9850, and cross-validated RMSE = 12.47 �b1 0.26 MPa, while reproducing flow curves and DRX kinetics.
infographic.watch_item: Performance depends on the quality and coverage of experimental training data, and the added physics constraints/latent fusion may be sensitive to parameter tuning outside the tested processing window.
infographic.paper_title: Physics-Informed Residual Deep Learning for Constitutive Modeling of Hot Deformation and Dynamic Recrystallization in a Mo-Rich $α+β$ Titanium Alloy

title: Decoupling Strain-Rate Sensitivity and Deformation Length Scale Effects in Neutron-Irradiated Tungsten: A Coupled Nano-Indentation, HR-EBSD and Crystal Plasticity Study
authors: Prashil S. Joshi, Bethany Jim, Chris Hardie, Angus Wilkinson, David EJ Armstrong, Suchandrima Das
abstract: Plastic deformation during strain-rate-controlled spherical nanoindentation is governed by the coupled evolution of constitutive strain-rate sensitivity and deformation length scale, making the intrinsic influence of strain rate difficult to isolate experimentally. This coupling is investigated in unirradiated and neutron-irradiated single-crystal tungsten using spherical nanoindentation, atomic force microscopy, high-resolution electron backscatter diffraction (HR-EBSD), and crystal plasticity finite element (CPFE) modeling. Nanoindentation experiments were performed at strain rates from 3.2e-5 to 3.2e-3 per second. AFM and HR-EBSD quantified surface pile-up, residual lattice strain, and geometrically necessary dislocation (GND) distributions. A strain-gradient CPFE framework incorporating thermally activated slip, GND hardening, irradiation-induced obstacle hardening, and strain-dependent softening was calibrated using a single experimental condition and validated across all remaining strain rates without further parameter adjustment. The validated model was then used to independently vary strain rate and indentation depth. Simulations show that strain rate primarily controls the stress required for thermally activated plastic flow, whereas indentation depth governs plastic-zone evolution, pile-up, and GND accumulation. Irradiation increases obstacle strength and promotes deformation localization while remaining consistent with a common thermally activated mechanism. The framework also predicts the compression response of a polycrystalline cube, demonstrating transferability across loading conditions and length scales, providing a robust basis for constitutive modeling of irradiation-hardened materials under transient loading.

infographic.key_insight: In neutron-irradiated tungsten, strain-rate sensitivity and deformation length scale can be separated: strain rate mainly raises the flow stress, while indentation depth mainly governs plastic-zone size, pile-up, and GND buildup.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This enables more reliable constitutive models for irradiation-hardened metals under transient loading, improving prediction of indentation, contact, and compression responses across scales.
method: Spherical nanoindentation over 3.2e-5 to 3.2e-3 s^-1
AFM and HR-EBSD to measure pile-up, residual strain, and GNDs
Strain-gradient CPFE with thermally activated slip, GND hardening, irradiation obstacles, and softening
infographic.evidence_signal: A single calibrated CPFE condition successfully validated against all other strain rates without retuning, and then reproduced independent variation of strain rate and indentation depth as well as polycrystal compression behavior.
infographic.watch_item: The model is calibrated on one experimental condition, so transfer to other irradiation states, temperatures, or loading paths should be checked before broad deployment.
infographic.paper_title: Decoupling Strain-Rate Sensitivity and Deformation Length Scale Effects in Neutron-Irradiated Tungsten: A Coupled Nano-Indentation, HR-EBSD and Crystal Plasticity Study

title: Modeling damage and fracture in additively manufactured polymeric triply periodic minimal surface lattices
authors: Abhishek Gupta, Aditya Konale, Ke Ma, Keven Alkhoury, Pradeep Guduru, Yuri Bazilevs
abstract: Architected triply periodic minimal surface (TPMS) lattices offer superior specific energy absorption, toughness, fatigue strength, and tunability. While recent advancements have established rate-dependent viscoplastic constitutive models to capture the complex nonlinear deformation response of additively manufactured polymeric TPMS structures, predicting fracture and the resulting structural failure remains a significant challenge. We address this by performing systematic experiments on unit cells and lattices of various sizes under tension, compression, and non-monotonic loading. The experiments inform the development of a new constitutive model that captures the damage and fracture behavior of polymeric TPMS lattices. We first implement a high-fidelity viscoplastic deformation constitutive model from Ma et al. (2026) into finite element software Abaqus/Explicit via a user material subroutine. We then propose a damage initiation criterion for amorphous polymers based on stored elastic energy and equivalent plastic strain. The damage model is implemented in Abaqus using gradient-damage framework following Konale and Srivastava(2025). The damage model and numerical simulation capability are quantitatively and qualitatively validated using experimental results for a unit cell under non-monotonic loading and lattices under tension. The proposed damage model and simulation capability enable in silico design of architected polymer structures.

infographic.key_insight: A new damage-and-fracture constitutive framework is developed to predict failure in additively manufactured polymer TPMS lattices by combining viscoplastic deformation, a polymer damage initiation criterion, and gradient damage evolution.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The model enables more reliable in silico design of architected polymer lattices for energy absorption and load-bearing applications where fracture governs performance.
method: Implemented a high-fidelity viscoplastic model in Abaqus/Explicit via UMAT.
Introduced a damage initiation criterion based on stored elastic energy and equivalent plastic strain.
Added a gradient-damage formulation and calibrated/validated it against experiments on TPMS unit cells and lattices.
infographic.evidence_signal: The model was quantitatively and qualitatively validated using experimental data from unit cells under non-monotonic loading and lattices under tension.
infographic.watch_item: The framework is calibrated for specific polymer TPMS geometries and loading paths, so transferability to other materials, architectures, or rate regimes may require revalidation.
infographic.paper_title: Modeling damage and fracture in additively manufactured polymeric triply periodic minimal surface lattices

title: Harnessing Machine Learning for Hybrid Constitutive Modelling of Viscoelastic Fluid Flows in Computational Rheology
authors: J. L. Cummings, C. Fernandes, F. Dong, M. A. Alves, M. S. N. Oliveira
abstract: Recent advances in data-driven modelling have highlighted the potential of hybrid approaches which combine Tensor Basis Neural Networks (TBNN) with Universal Differential Equations (UDE) to discover frame-invariant, non-linear viscoelastic constitutive models. These hybrid models enable the creation of digital twins for complex viscoelastic fluids, offering direct transferability to computational fluid dynamics simulations. In this work, we introduce a reduced dimensional tensor basis formulation that enhances both the physical consistency of the learned representations with respect to the training data and the numerical stability of subsequent simulations. The UDE architecture is embedded into an open-source finite volume solver in which the constitutive response is generated dynamically at runtime based on local fluid flow conditions. Training on synthetic datasets generated using a range of well established viscoelastic models in oscillatory shear flows alone, the performance of the resulting UDEs is evaluated under extrapolation to unseen conditions and flow-types. These include deploying the UDEs in viscometric extensional flows as well as 2D and 3D benchmark flows, such as the 4:1 sudden contraction and cross-slot, providing a quantitative analysis of their capabilities, limitations and failure modes. The proposed reduced-basis framework enables data-efficient discovery of frame-invariant constitutive models that generalise beyond their training regime, capturing key flow features such as the onset and growth of flow-induced elastic instabilities in strong extensional flows even though trained solely on shear data. Quantitative accuracy decreases as extrapolation increases, but incorporating first normal stress difference information further improves quantitative accuracy and extends predictive fidelity to higher Deborah numbers.

infographic.key_insight: A reduced-basis hybrid TBNN-UDE framework can learn frame-invariant viscoelastic constitutive laws from shear data and transfer them to more complex flows.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This enables data-driven digital twins for viscoelastic fluids that can be embedded directly in CFD solvers for predictive rheology and flow design.
method: Train a tensor-basis neural network coupled to a universal differential equation constitutive model.
Use a reduced dimensional tensor basis to improve physical consistency and numerical stability.
Embed the learned constitutive response in an open-source finite volume solver for runtime evaluation.
infographic.evidence_signal: The models were trained on synthetic oscillatory shear datasets from established viscoelastic laws and then tested by extrapolating to extensional, contraction, and cross-slot flows, including 2D and 3D benchmarks.
infographic.watch_item: Accuracy drops as the model moves further outside the shear-training regime, so extrapolation to strong flow-type changes and high Deborah numbers remains a key risk.
infographic.paper_title: Harnessing Machine Learning for Hybrid Constitutive Modelling of Viscoelastic Fluid Flows in Computational Rheology

title: Model-Informed Joint Material-Structural Optimization of Hard-Magnetic Soft Materials
authors: Ian Galloway, Prashant K. Jha
abstract: This work develops a model-informed framework for predictive analysis and optimal design of hard-magnetic soft materials (hMSMs). These materials undergo contact-free, field-driven deformation, making them attractive for soft robotics, adaptive structures, and bio-inspired systems. Accurate prediction requires effective structure--property relations, while optimal design requires simultaneous control of structural density, magnetic particle distribution, and remanent magnetization direction. To address these issues, this work makes two main contributions. First, classical rigid-inclusion relations, a Hill self-consistent relation, and constrained-kinematics models are placed into a unified effective shear-modulus framework for particle-filled elastomers. With one default control relation, seven shear-modulus relations are combined with three strain-energy density functions to obtain 21 constitutive models. The results show that the strain-energy density form has a relatively small effect for the actuation problems considered, whereas the effective shear-modulus relation can significantly affect deformation when magnetic material overlaps with highly deforming regions. Experimental stress--strain data are then used to select a representative shear-modulus relation, with the Mooney relation giving the best overall agreement. Second, using the selected constitutive model, a joint material--structural optimization framework is developed for simultaneous design of structural density, magnetic particle volume fraction, and remanent magnetization direction. Rotational, translational, and restorative examples show that the framework handles different active design fields, objectives, and single- or multi-load-case formulations, producing non-intuitive hMSM designs with prescribed deformation responses. The framework is implemented in the open-source \texttt{CEADpx/top\_optim} repository.

infographic.key_insight: A unified constitutive modeling and optimization framework enables predictive design of hard-magnetic soft materials by coupling effective shear-modulus relations with structural, magnetic, and remanence design variables.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This supports the design of soft robotic and adaptive structures that deform contact-free under magnetic fields with better control over material layout and actuation response.
method: Unified 7 effective shear-modulus relations with 3 strain-energy densities to form 21 constitutive models
Compared model predictions for actuation problems to identify the most suitable material law
Built joint optimization over structural density, particle volume fraction, and remanent magnetization direction
infographic.evidence_signal: Experimental stress-strain data were used to screen the constitutive models, and the Mooney relation gave the best overall agreement in the reported comparisons.
infographic.watch_item: The actuation response can depend strongly on the chosen effective shear-modulus relation when magnetic material overlaps highly deforming regions, so constitutive-model selection remains important.
infographic.paper_title: Model-Informed Joint Material-Structural Optimization of Hard-Magnetic Soft Materials

title: Modeling the Fatigue Behavior of Amorphous Polymers
authors: Valeriy V. Ginzburg, Oleg V. Gendelman, Alessio Zaccone
abstract: Prediction of material durability is both very important and very difficult. In many cases, material durability is measured by subjecting a sample to repeating oscillatory cycles (in shear or tension-compression) until it fails in either ductile or brittle fashion. Typically, the stress amplitude is denoted S, and the number of cycles N, so the resulting dependence is known as the SN-curve. For many materials, SN curve has been shown to obey the empirical Basquin's law, N = AS^(-m), where the prefactor A was a function of the temperature, load frequency, and sample history, and the power law m was a real number, usually between 3 and 12. Here, we derive the Basquin's law using a linearized version of the Long's plasticity model and demonstrate that within this framework, m = 3. We also derive the expression for the prefactor A. Finally, we show that our theory successfully describes experimental data for three amorphous polymers, PS, PMMA, and PVC.

infographic.key_insight: A linearized Long plasticity model derives Basquin’s fatigue law for amorphous polymers and predicts a universal exponent m = 3.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This gives engineers a physics-based way to estimate fatigue life from stress amplitude, helping durability design for amorphous polymers such as PS, PMMA, and PVC.
method: Linearize Long’s plasticity model for cyclic loading.
Relate stress amplitude S to fatigue life N and derive N = A S^(-m).
Obtain the prefactor A and identify m = 3 within the framework.
infographic.evidence_signal: The theory is compared against experimental fatigue data for three amorphous polymers: PS, PMMA, and PVC.
infographic.watch_item: The model is derived from a linearized framework, so its accuracy may be limited outside the stress ranges and material conditions covered by the experimental data.
infographic.paper_title: Modeling the Fatigue Behavior of Amorphous Polymers

title: An Order-One Lower Bound on the Error of Scalable Generalized Multiscale Finite Element Space Constructions
authors: Changqing Ye
abstract: Several coefficient-adapted methods provide optimal-order approximation for elliptic equations with rough coefficients. Prominent examples include localized orthogonal decomposition, multiscale spectral GFEM, and constraint energy-minimizing GMsFEM. Their proven accuracy, however, is obtained by allowing the localization radius or the local spectral dimension to grow as the coarse scale \(H\) tends to zero. Classical MsFEM has an FEM-like local construction, but its available analysis does not give a coefficient-uniform \(\bigO(H)\) energy estimate over the full bounded-contrast measurable coefficient class. Motivated by this gap, we formalize an FEM-like notion of structural scalability. A chosen spatially local basis has uniformly bounded overlap, hence \(\bigO(1)\) stiffness entries per row, and every anchored local span uses coefficient information from only \(\bigO(1)\) coarse-element layers. We prove that no deterministic construction satisfying fixed bounds on the support radius, coefficient-information radius, and local multiplicity can converge uniformly over the coefficient class. In fact, its worst-case \(L^2\)-to-energy Galerkin error remains bounded below by a positive constant independent of \(H\). The lower bound is established using a fixed finite family of smooth periodic coefficients and smooth right-hand sides. The proof combines coefficients that coincide on local patches, a finite-dimensional approximation lower bound for corrector fields, a positive-density mesh argument, and strong periodic corrector convergence. Thus uniform optimal accuracy requires at least one local construction parameter to grow or requires coefficient information beyond the fixed-visibility model.

infographic.key_insight: Any FEM-like generalized multiscale space with fixed local support and fixed coefficient visibility cannot achieve uniformly vanishing error for all bounded-contrast rough coefficients; its worst-case error stays O(1).
infographic.engineering_relevance: This shows that truly scalable multiscale FEM surrogates must relax locality or increase coefficient information as the mesh is refined to retain reliable accuracy.
method: Defines scalability via bounded overlap, fixed support radius, and fixed coefficient-information radius.
Proves an order-one lower bound for deterministic local space constructions in the full measurable coefficient class.
Uses periodic coefficient counterexamples, local patch indistinguishability, and corrector-field approximation arguments.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports a theoretical worst-case L2-to-energy Galerkin error lower bound that remains positive and independent of H, established with a finite family of smooth periodic coefficients and smooth right-hand sides.
infographic.watch_item: The result is a worst-case impossibility theorem, so practical performance may still be good on restricted coefficient families or with adaptive/nonlocal enrichment.
infographic.paper_title: An Order-One Lower Bound on the Error of Scalable Generalized Multiscale Finite Element Space Constructions

title: Microscopic constitutive theory of stress overshoot, yielding, and strain hardening in amorphous materials
authors: Ankit Singh, Valeriy V. Ginzburg, Alessio Zaccone
abstract: We develop a microscopic constitutive theory for the nonlinear deformation of metallic and polymer glasses based on nonaffine elasticity coupled to irreversible many-body relaxation. The theory predicts the full stress--strain response, from linear elasticity through stress overshoot and yielding to steady plastic flow. We show that stress overshoot originates from the competition between a nonaffine elastic instability induced by strain-driven loss of mechanical connectivity at the atomic/molecular level, and viscous dissipation associated with structural relaxation. For polymer glasses, finite chain extensibility naturally accounts for strain hardening at large deformation. The stretched-exponential relaxation exponent is obtained independently from stress or modulus relaxation measurements and provides the primary dynamical input to the theory. Using a small set of physically meaningful parameters, the model quantitatively reproduces experimental stress--strain curves for metallic glasses, polycarbonate, PMMA, and epoxy resins over a broad range of strain rates. These results establish a unified microscopic framework linking relaxation dynamics, yielding, plastic flow, and strain hardening in amorphous solids.

infographic.key_insight: A microscopic constitutive model links nonaffine elasticity and structural relaxation to explain stress overshoot, yielding, steady flow, and polymer strain hardening in amorphous solids.
infographic.engineering_relevance: It provides a unified, physically grounded way to predict full stress--strain curves of metallic and polymer glasses across strain rates using a small parameter set.
method: Nonaffine elasticity captures strain-driven loss of mechanical connectivity and the onset of elastic instability.
Irreversible many-body relaxation with a stretched-exponential exponent governs viscous dissipation and flow.
Finite chain extensibility is added for polymer glasses to reproduce strain hardening at large deformation.
infographic.evidence_signal: The model is validated by quantitatively reproducing experimental stress--strain curves for metallic glasses, polycarbonate, PMMA, and epoxy resins over a broad range of strain rates.
infographic.watch_item: The framework depends on fitted physically meaningful parameters and an empirically determined relaxation exponent, so predictive accuracy may vary if those inputs are unavailable or outside the tested material classes.
infographic.paper_title: Microscopic constitutive theory of stress overshoot, yielding, and strain hardening in amorphous materials

title: Trajectory-Aware Flow Matching for Topology Optimisation
authors: Shusheng Xiao, Jinshuai Bai, Hyogu Jeong, Yunfei Xi, Yilin Gui, YuanTong Gu
abstract: Topology optimisation (TO) often requires repeated finite element analysis and sensitivity-based material updates, which can be costly when multiple candidate designs are needed under varying physical and design conditions. Generative TO offers a route to rapid design exploration, but existing models may rely on adversarial training, long reverse-diffusion sampling, or external guidance to maintain structural feasibility and physical consistency. This study develops a flow matching-based topology optimisation (FMTO) framework for conditional topology generation. Linear FMTO is first formulated as an endpoint-based baseline by interpolating between a Gaussian source field and the BESO reference topology. To introduce mechanically meaningful intermediate states, a trajectory-aware FMTO formulation is proposed, where volume-fraction-indexed BESO states are used to construct the probability path and target velocity field. This incorporates physics-guided optimisation history into generative flow learning without adding inference-time optimisation. A path--velocity mismatch analysis explains why moderate trajectory weighting can improve generation stability, whereas excessive guidance may over-constrain the learned transport. Numerical examples show that FMTO generates diverse topology candidates with improved compliance-related performance, volume-fraction satisfaction, topology fidelity, and substantially fewer sampling steps than a diffusion-based baseline. Under limited training data, trajectory-aware FMTO achieves the best overall performance with a moderate trajectory weight. Studies on trajectory-anchor density and three-dimensional topology generation further demonstrate the influence of path design and the applicability of the proposed framework beyond two-dimensional problems.

infographic.key_insight: Trajectory-aware flow matching injects physics-guided optimization history into conditional topology generation, enabling faster and more faithful designs than endpoint-only generative training.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The framework can speed up topology optimization workflows by producing multiple feasible candidate structures with fewer sampling steps and without costly inference-time optimization.
method: Linear FMTO: interpolate between Gaussian noise and a BESO reference topology.
Trajectory-aware FMTO: build the probability path and target velocity from volume-fraction-indexed BESO states.
Train a flow model to match the transport field for conditional topology generation.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports improved compliance-related performance, volume-fraction satisfaction, and topology fidelity, with substantially fewer sampling steps than a diffusion baseline, including strong results under limited training data.
infographic.watch_item: Too much trajectory weighting can over-constrain transport and reduce generation stability, so the path design and anchor density need careful tuning.
infographic.paper_title: Trajectory-Aware Flow Matching for Topology Optimisation

title: An Adaptive and Physics-Preserving Multiscale Method for Two-Phase Flow Simulations in High-Contrast Heterogeneous Porous Media
authors: Junhao Huang, Eric Chung, Wing Tat Leung
abstract: In this paper, we propose an adaptive physics-preserving multiscale method for incompressible and immiscible two-phase flow in high-contrast porous media. The method couples a physics-preserving implicit-pressure explicit-saturation scheme (P-IMPES) with the mixed constraint energy minimizing generalized multiscale finite element method. The core algorithmic component is an adaptive update strategy for the saturation-dependent coefficient. Since the effective permeability \(κ_n=λ_t(S_w^n)K\) depends on the evolving saturation through the total mobility, we introduce an adaptive update algorithm that monitors the variation of the mobility-weighted coefficient and regenerates the multiscale spaces only when a prescribed tolerance is exceeded. A local postprocessing step is further used to recover fine-grid mass conservation. The analysis is a central part of the paper. We prove local conservation for both phases, the unbiased property of the phase formulation, and bounds preservation under a suitable CFL condition. For the advection-dominated case, we establish velocity and saturation error estimates, which clearly identify the contributions from the adaptive tolerance, the coarse mesh size, the spectral approximation, and the front-layer error. Numerical experiments on different high-contrast permeability fields confirm the physical properties of the method and show that smaller adaptive tolerances improve the saturation approximation while avoiding unnecessary updates of the multiscale spaces.

infographic.key_insight: An adaptive multiscale P-IMPES scheme updates coarse-scale physics only when saturation-driven mobility changes become significant, preserving conservation and accuracy in high-contrast two-phase porous flow.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This enables efficient, physically reliable simulation of incompressible immiscible flow in heterogeneous reservoirs while reducing unnecessary multiscale space rebuilds.
method: Couples physics-preserving IMPES with mixed constraint energy-minimizing multiscale finite elements.
Adapts multiscale basis updates using a tolerance on the mobility-weighted permeability change.
Applies local postprocessing to recover fine-grid mass conservation.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports analytical guarantees and numerical experiments on multiple high-contrast permeability fields showing improved saturation accuracy with smaller adaptive tolerances and fewer redundant updates.
infographic.watch_item: Performance depends on a suitable tolerance and CFL condition; too aggressive updating or violating stability constraints may affect bounds preservation and accuracy.
infographic.paper_title: An Adaptive and Physics-Preserving Multiscale Method for Two-Phase Flow Simulations in High-Contrast Heterogeneous Porous Media

title: Accounting for Hysteresis and Eddy Currents in Finite Element Simulations of Ferromagnetic Laminated Cores using a Recurrent Neural Network
authors: Florent Purnode, Louis Denis, François Henrotte, Gilles Louppe, Christophe Geuzaine
abstract: Incorporating hysteresis and eddy currents into finite element simulations of laminated-core electrical machines is computationally challenging. Resolving the fields inside the laminations at each integration point and at every nonlinear iteration leads to computational costs several orders of magnitude higher than anhysteretic simulations, making such approaches impractical for design applications. Conversely, simplified models accounting only for magnetic saturation are becoming increasingly inadequate as electrical machine topologies and operating conditions grow in complexity. In this context, machine learning surrogate modeling has emerged as a promising alternative, offering efficient and accurate approximations of complex electromagnetic behaviors. In this paper, a recurrent neural network is trained as a surrogate of a laminated-core material model for an isotropic laminated core, and is integrated into realistic two-dimensional magnetodynamic finite element simulations based on a magnetic vector potential formulation. The proposed approach achieves excellent agreement with the reference laminated-core model while limiting the computational cost to about twice that of an anhysteretic simulation. By training the recurrent neural network on a sufficiently diverse set of artificially generated magnetic field sequences designed to mimic those encountered in electrical machine simulations, the proposed approach can be readily applied across a wide range of finite element simulations. Furthermore, the trained surrogate model is provided as a standalone component that can be easily incorporated into existing computational frameworks. It is publicly available at https://gitlab.onelab.info/getdp/lamnet.

infographic.key_insight: A recurrent neural network can act as a fast surrogate for laminated ferromagnetic core hysteresis and eddy-current behavior inside finite element simulations.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This enables much more realistic electrical machine and magnetic device modeling at a cost close to an anhysteretic simulation, making design workflows more practical.
method: Train an RNN on diverse synthetic magnetic-field sequences that mimic machine operating conditions.
Embed the trained surrogate into 2D magnetodynamic finite element simulations using a magnetic vector potential formulation.
Use the surrogate to reproduce a reference laminated-core material model without resolving every lamination in detail.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports excellent agreement with the reference laminated-core model while keeping runtime to about twice that of an anhysteretic simulation.
infographic.watch_item: Performance may depend on how well the training sequences cover the operating conditions encountered in real finite element studies.
infographic.paper_title: Accounting for Hysteresis and Eddy Currents in Finite Element Simulations of Ferromagnetic Laminated Cores using a Recurrent Neural Network

title: Fully discrete least-squares splitting scheme for the Monge-Ampère equation: finite element analysis and convergence
authors: Anna Peruso
abstract: The least-squares splitting algorithm for the Monge-Ampère equation has been used successfully in computations for several years, but a convergence theory for fully discrete splitting schemes of this type has remained unavailable. In this work, we introduce and analyze a finite element framework for smooth solutions of the Dirichlet Monge-Ampère equation in two dimensions. The proposed schemes combine a discrete Hessian reconstruction with a local projection onto the determinant constraint. Under a discrete Miranda-Talenti estimate and standard approximation properties of the Hessian reconstruction, we prove local convergence of the iterative scheme and optimal-order convergence of its limit to the exact solution in an $H^2$-type norm. We verify the estimates for conforming $C^1$ schemes, including the Argyris element, and for $C^0$-interior penalty and DG schemes of degree at least three; quadratic $C^0$-interior penalty and DG schemes are also covered when $|u|_{H^3(Ω)}$ is sufficiently small. To the best of our knowledge, these discretizations have not previously been proposed or analyzed for least-squares splitting methods. Numerical experiments confirm the theoretical convergence rates.

infographic.key_insight: A fully discrete least-squares splitting framework can be proven convergent for the 2D Monge-Ampère equation when combined with finite element Hessian reconstruction and a local determinant projection.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This gives a rigorous path to stable, high-order finite element solvers for nonlinear PDEs used in geometry, optimal transport, and related computational mechanics problems.
method: Reconstruct the Hessian with finite elements.
Project locally onto the Monge-Ampère determinant constraint.
Use discrete Miranda-Talenti estimates to prove local convergence and optimal H^2-type convergence.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports optimal-order convergence of the limit solution and numerical experiments that confirm the predicted convergence rates.
infographic.watch_item: Convergence is local and relies on a discrete Miranda-Talenti estimate; some lower-order C0-IP/DG cases require sufficiently small |u|_{H^3(Ω)}.
infographic.paper_title: Fully discrete least-squares splitting scheme for the Monge-Ampère equation: finite element analysis and convergence

title: TopoAgent: A Self-Evolving Topological Agent for Multimodal Scientific Reasoning
authors: Mingze Xu, Yinghui Li, Jiayi Kuang, Zhanhui Kang, Di Yin, Ying Shen
abstract: While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) excel in general tasks, rigorous scientific reasoning remains challenging due to the limitations of monolithic, linear planning. Such sequential designs often suffer from visual-semantic misalignment, long-context hallucinations, and brittle execution under fixed task granularity. We propose TopoAgent, a self-evolving topological framework that replaces linear trajectories with dynamic, state-isolated graph evolution. TopoAgent first employs a front-end decomposer to fracture complex queries into visually-grounded atoms. These atoms are organized into a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) based on their dependencies, enabling strict context isolation to shield the reasoning engine from irrelevant historical noise. Furthermore, we introduce adaptive atomic fission, which dynamically splits bottleneck nodes into finer-grained sub-atoms at runtime when tool capability boundaries are exceeded. Extensive experiments across mathematics, physics, and chemistry benchmarks demonstrate that TopoAgent significantly outperforms state-of-the-art linear agent frameworks, providing a robust, noise-resistant, and self-correcting paradigm for autonomous scientific reasoning.

infographic.key_insight: TopoAgent replaces brittle linear multimodal reasoning with a self-evolving DAG of isolated atomic tasks, improving scientific problem solving through dynamic decomposition and runtime fission.
infographic.engineering_relevance: For fracture and damage workflows, this suggests a more robust way to structure complex multimodal analysis pipelines so each subproblem stays context-grounded and less prone to hallucination.
method: Front-end decomposer fractures complex queries into visually grounded atoms.
Atoms are arranged in a directed acyclic graph to enforce dependency-aware execution and strict context isolation.
Adaptive atomic fission splits bottleneck nodes into finer sub-atoms when tool limits are exceeded.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports extensive evaluation across mathematics, physics, and chemistry benchmarks, with TopoAgent outperforming state-of-the-art linear agent frameworks.
infographic.watch_item: Performance may depend on correct graph decomposition and runtime splitting decisions, so implementation complexity and failure modes in atomization remain a practical concern.
infographic.paper_title: TopoAgent: A Self-Evolving Topological Agent for Multimodal Scientific Reasoning

title: Numerical and experimental framework for bending elasticity of highly flexible slender structures
authors: Shunsuke Nomura, Satsuki Shibuya, Isamu Hashiguchi, Ryuichi Tarumi, Tomohiko G. Sano
abstract: Slender structures are highly flexible, spanning several orders of magnitude in length scale. Their deformation depends on the slenderness of their cross sections, highlighting that the elasticity and geometry of structures are intrinsically coupled. The deformation of the cross-section becomes significant, particularly when tubes and pipes are subjected to bending, known as the Brazier instability. Although the bending performance of slender structures is quantified experimentally using a canonical three-point bending test, their numerical counterparts remain under-explored because complex contact mechanics must be implemented in simulations. In this study, we develop a computational framework to simulate experimental three-point bending tests using a hybrid material point method (hybrid-MPM) approach, which integrates Lagrangian finite element and Eulerian finite difference frameworks. We adapt our framework to elastic tubes and tape springs as canonical examples that exhibit characteristic bending deformation in which the cross-sectional and lengthwise bending are coupled. The predictions of numerical simulations are validated against desktop experiments and classical theory. The excellent agreement between the simulation and the experiments implies that the hybrid-MPM framework provides a robust computational framework for predicting the large deformation of structures involving complex contact, such as soft robots and deployable structures.

infographic.key_insight: A hybrid material point method can accurately capture large bending deformations of slender structures by resolving the coupled interaction between cross-sectional distortion and lengthwise flexure.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This provides a practical simulation framework for predicting bending behavior in flexible tubes, tape springs, soft robots, and deployable structures where contact and instability are difficult to model.
method: Hybrid-MPM couples Lagrangian finite elements with Eulerian finite differences.
Simulates experimental three-point bending tests with complex contact.
Applied to elastic tubes and tape springs exhibiting Brazier-type coupling.
infographic.evidence_signal: The simulations were validated against desktop experiments and classical theory, showing excellent agreement in bending response and deformation patterns.
infographic.watch_item: Accuracy may depend on careful contact handling and calibration for highly nonlinear large-deformation cases.
infographic.paper_title: Numerical and experimental framework for bending elasticity of highly flexible slender structures

title: Towards end-to-end optimization in multimaterial 3D printing
authors: Xue-Ling Luo, Steven Yang, Jingye Tan, Robert F. Shepherd, Noy Cohen, Nikolaos Bouklas
abstract: Multimaterial 3D printing enables the fabrication of functionally graded components, but optimizing their spatial material distribution alongside structural topology remains a formidable challenge due to high-dimensional design spaces and complex constitutive modeling. This paper presents an end-to-end computational framework integrating sparsified physics-augmented neural networks with finite-element-based topology optimization. By extracting closed-form, composition-aware hyperelastic constitutive laws from experimental data, this approach facilitates exact symbolic differentiation via the adjoint state method implemented with FEniCSx, efficiently circumventing the bottlenecks of applying neural network constitutive models. This pipeline is deployed on soft robotic gripper applications, demonstrating continuous composition optimization for highly anisotropic contact responses, and the concurrent optimization of macroscopic topology and material distribution under non-failure stretch constraints. This methodology could replace laborious empirical prototyping, establishing interpretable machine-learning models as practical, robust design primitives for advanced multimaterial additive manufacturing.

infographic.key_insight: The paper turns multimaterial 3D printing design into an end-to-end optimization problem by learning closed-form, composition-aware constitutive laws and coupling them directly to topology optimization.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This enables simultaneous optimization of shape and material distribution for soft robotic structures, reducing reliance on slow trial-and-error prototyping.
method: Train sparsified physics-augmented neural networks on experimental data to recover interpretable hyperelastic laws.
Use symbolic differentiation and an adjoint method in FEniCSx to couple the constitutive model with finite-element topology optimization.
Apply the framework to soft robotic grippers for continuous composition design and topology/material co-optimization under stretch constraints.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports demonstration on soft robotic gripper applications, including continuous composition optimization and concurrent topology/material optimization under non-failure stretch limits.
infographic.watch_item: The approach depends on extracting accurate closed-form constitutive laws from data, so model fidelity and identifiability may be challenging for more complex materials or sparse experiments.
infographic.paper_title: Towards end-to-end optimization in multimaterial 3D printing

title: Structure-preserving Lagrange-multiplier methods for mean curvature flow and their error bounds
authors: Balázs Kovács, Buyang Li, Christian Lubich
abstract: We propose and analyze a class of evolving surface finite element methods for mean curvature flow of closed surfaces using Lagrange multipliers for preserving the energy-decreasing structure. The algorithm is based on the solution-driven formulation using Huisken's evolution equations for the normal vector and the mean curvature. The time discretizations use linearly implicit backward difference formulas (BDF) for the parabolic geometric variables and implicit Adams updates for the nodal positions. The approach also accommodates artificial tangential velocities of minimal-deformation-rate type. The resulting fully discrete algorithms are area-decreasing at every time step, with a prescribed decay rate determined by the computed mean curvature. We prove local existence and uniqueness of the discrete Lagrange multiplier and convergence of a simplified Newton iteration for its computation under weak regularity assumptions. Under stronger regularity assumptions, as used in the convergence theory for the underlying evolving surface finite element method, we derive optimal-order error bounds of order $h^k+τ^q$ in the $H^1$-norm for finite elements of polynomial degree $k\ge 2$ and $q$-step BDF and $q$-step implicit Adams methods with $2\le q\le 5$, both without and with minimal-deformation-rate tangential motion. Numerical experiments for mean curvature flow of a sphere confirm the predicted convergence rates and show that the Lagrange-multiplier correction entails only a small computational overhead that is essentially independent of the mesh size and the time step size.

infographic.key_insight: The paper adds a Lagrange-multiplier correction to evolving surface finite element methods so mean curvature flow stays structure-preserving and area-decreasing at each time step.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This gives a stable, high-order way to simulate curvature-driven surface evolution with provable error bounds, which is useful for geometry processing and interface evolution in computational mechanics.
method: Uses a solution-driven ESFEM formulation based on Huisken-type evolution equations for the normal vector and mean curvature.
Applies linearly implicit BDF schemes for geometric variables and implicit Adams updates for nodal positions.
Includes optional minimal-deformation-rate tangential motion while preserving discrete area decay.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports optimal-order H1 error bounds of order h^k+τ^q and numerical sphere tests that match the predicted convergence rates with only small overhead.
infographic.watch_item: The strongest guarantees rely on stronger regularity assumptions, and practical performance still depends on accurate discrete Lagrange-multiplier solves and Newton iteration convergence.
infographic.paper_title: Structure-preserving Lagrange-multiplier methods for mean curvature flow and their error bounds

title: Neural operators solve inverse problems for constitutive model discovery
authors: Moritz Flaschel, Burigede Liu, Ellen Kuhl
abstract: Characterizing the mechanical response of materials traditionally requires solving optimization problems in which model parameters are calibrated or trained to minimize the discrepancy between model predictions and experimental data. This process can be computationally expensive and time-consuming. To overcome this limitation, we propose two neural operator architectures that directly map experimentally measured data to the constitutive functions governing the mechanical response of the material: Physics-Augmented Neural Operators (PANO) and Constitutive Artificial Neural Operators (CANO). The proposed neural operators approximate the mapping between the infinite-dimensional input space of full-field displacement measurements and net reaction forces, and the infinite-dimensional output space of hyperelastic strain-energy density functions. The displacement fields are encoded through Laplacian eigenfunctions to obtain discretization-independent and noise-robust predictions. Our framework constrains the output space to physically admissible material models that satisfy fundamental physical requirements by design. The neural operators are trained on simulated data tuples of displacement fields and reaction forces for a range of material models. Once trained, the neural operators enable near-instantaneous material characterization and require only a single forward pass to infer the strain-energy density function from a given experimental dataset. We test the predictive power of the neural operators for unseen data, noisy data, data with missing information, data from different spatial discretizations, and data from geometries of different sizes.

infographic.key_insight: Neural operators can learn the inverse mapping from full-field displacement and force data directly to physically admissible constitutive laws, enabling rapid material model discovery.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This could replace slow, iterative calibration with near-instantaneous constitutive identification from experimental measurements in solid mechanics.
method: PANO adds physics constraints while mapping measured displacements and reaction forces to strain-energy functions.
CANO learns constitutive functions in a constrained output space to ensure physically admissible material behavior.
Laplacian eigenfunction encoding makes the approach more robust to discretization changes and noise.
infographic.evidence_signal: The models were trained on simulated displacement-force tuples across multiple material models and tested on unseen, noisy, partially missing, and differently discretized data as well as different geometries.
infographic.watch_item: Performance may depend on how well the training simulations cover the range of real experimental conditions and material behaviors.
infographic.paper_title: Neural operators solve inverse problems for constitutive model discovery

title: A Unified Conceptual Framework for Gravitational Instabilities: From Latent System State to Failure
authors: Jérome Faillettaz
abstract: Gravitational instabilities are among the most widespread natural hazards and are expected to become increasingly significant under ongoing environmental change. Despite substantial advances in process understanding, monitoring, and modeling, predicting the timing of failure remains a fundamental challenge because instability emerges from complex interactions operating across multiple spatial and temporal scales. Existing approaches often focus on specific processes, forcing mechanisms, or observational signatures, resulting in a fragmented view of how systems evolve toward failure. This article propose a unified conceptual framework in which gravitational instabilities are interpreted as the progressive evolution of a latent system state controlled by damage accumulation, stress redistribution and external forcing to catastrophic failure. Within this perspective, failure emerges from the continuous interplay between internal system dynamics and external forcing, while predictability depends on our ability to infer the evolving state of the system from incomplete and indirect observations. The framework provides a common structure linking physical processes, observable manifestations, monitoring strategies, modeling approaches, and forecasting methods across a wide range of gravitational hazards. By integrating concepts from geomechanics, fracture mechanics, statistical physics, and data-driven sciences, the proposed framework shifts the focus from the search for universal precursors toward the reconstruction of evolving system states and their proximity to instability. It offers a unifying perspective for understanding failure processes and developing more robust approaches to hazard assessment and early warning.

infographic.key_insight: Gravitational failures are best understood as a latent system state that evolves through damage, stress redistribution, and external forcing until catastrophic collapse occurs.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This framework helps engineers unify monitoring, modeling, and early-warning efforts by focusing on reconstructing a system’s evolving proximity to failure rather than hunting for universal precursors.
method: Model failure as progressive evolution of an internal latent state
Link damage accumulation, stress redistribution, and forcing across scales
Infer state from incomplete observations using physical and data-driven methods
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract presents a conceptual synthesis rather than a new dataset or quantitative validation, emphasizing cross-disciplinary structure over measured performance.
infographic.watch_item: Its practical value depends on how reliably incomplete observations can be translated into the true evolving state, which may be difficult in heterogeneous natural systems.
infographic.paper_title: A Unified Conceptual Framework for Gravitational Instabilities: From Latent System State to Failure

title: A structure-preserving Numerical Method for the Compressible Resistive-Hall-MHD System
authors: Murtazo Nazarov, Rafael Rodriguez-Velasco, Ignacio Tomas
abstract: In this paper, we present a structure-preserving method for the compressible resistive Hall-magnetohydrodynamics (MHD) model. The differential operator is split into two parts: a hydrodynamic part consisting of the compressible Euler equations, and a magnetic part consisting of a system coupling the Lorentz force and the induction equation. The method uses continuous Lagrange elements for the Euler part and a curl-conforming finite element space for the magnetic part. The hydrodynamic part preserves the positivity of the density and internal energy, the conservation of total energy, and the minimum principle for the specific entropy. Owing to the choice of finite elements, the magnetic part preserves the divergence involution constraint. The fluid part is solved using explicit strong-stability-preserving Runge-Kutta (SSP-RK) methods, whereas the magnetic part is solved by Crank-Nicholson method, which requires using Newton's method. Coercivity estimates for the Jacobian of the corresponding Newton iteration are presented. We introduce a high-order artificial resistivity to improve the conditioning of the nonlinear residual and the invertibility of the Jacobian. Several challenging benchmarks, including a smooth whistler wave, the Orszag-Tang vortex for comparing resistive MHD with resistive Hall-MHD, and a magnetic reconnection problem, are solved to validate the robustness and accuracy of the method.

infographic.key_insight: The paper proposes a split, structure-preserving finite element scheme for compressible resistive Hall-MHD that maintains key physical invariants while handling the fluid and magnetic dynamics with tailored solvers.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This is relevant for simulation workflows in plasma, fusion, and electromagnetic flow systems where robustness, conservation, and divergence control are critical.
method: Split operator into hydrodynamic Euler and magnetic Lorentz/induction parts
Use continuous Lagrange elements for fluid variables and curl-conforming elements for magnetic fields
Solve fluid with explicit SSP-RK and magnetic update with Crank-Nicolson plus Newton iterations
infographic.evidence_signal: The method is validated on smooth whistler waves, Orszag-Tang vortex tests, and a magnetic reconnection benchmark, showing robustness and accuracy across challenging cases.
infographic.watch_item: The magnetic solve relies on Newton iterations and an added artificial resistivity term, which may affect implementation complexity and tuning.
infographic.paper_title: A structure-preserving Numerical Method for the Compressible Resistive-Hall-MHD System

title: Uncertainty-Aware Crack Growth Forecasting via Conditional Denoising Diffusion Models for Phase-Field Fracture
authors: Jahnavi Krishna Koda, S. M. Mallikarjunaiah
abstract: The accurate prediction of brittle crack initiation, propagation, and complex topological evolution remains computationally prohibitive when utilizing traditional high-fidelity phase-field finite element methods. To overcome these computational bottlenecks, a physics-informed conditional Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM) is proposed for the full-field spatiotemporal forecasting of fracture evolution across diverse loading regimes and energy decomposition methods. The generative architecture is conditioned on rolling historical damage states and explicitly derived kinematic proxies -- phase-field velocity and gradient magnitude -- ensuring temporal coherence without non-physical artifacts. The principal contribution is spatially-localized uncertainty quantification without modification to the trained model. Ensemble variance concentrates at crack branching junctions ($σ_\mathrm{max} = 0.222$ at Y-junction bifurcations; zero high-uncertainty pixels in four deterministic propagation cases), while the high-$σ$ tail identifies high-error predictions with 90\% precision -- an 18-fold improvement over random selection. One-step crack tip localization achieves sub-pixel accuracy (0.12 px mean error) across both held-out validation subsets (shear-star and tension-spect), confirming cross-regime generalization. In closed-loop autoregressive rollout over 50 steps, the DDPM maintains Dice = 0.929 $\pm$ 0.010 while a deterministic U-Net collapses to Dice = 0.423 under error accumulation, a 2.2$\times$ gap that establishes the value of stochastic re-sampling for long-horizon stability. Per-step inference requires approximately 3.6 s on an H100 GPU, approximately 28$\times$ faster than the FEM reference and 1{,}000$\times$ slower than a deterministic U-Net a cost that buys the stochastic diversity enabling uncertainty quantification.

infographic.key_insight: A physics-informed conditional diffusion model can forecast phase-field crack evolution while preserving temporal coherence and providing localized uncertainty estimates without changing the trained network.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This offers a practical surrogate for expensive fracture FEM simulations, enabling faster long-horizon crack growth prediction with uncertainty maps to support engineering decisions.
method: Condition the DDPM on rolling damage history plus kinematic proxies (phase-field velocity and gradient magnitude).
Generate autoregressive full-field fracture states to predict crack initiation, propagation, and branching.
Use ensemble variance from stochastic sampling to localize uncertainty and flag likely error regions.
infographic.evidence_signal: The model achieved 0.12 px mean crack-tip error on held-out cases, 90% precision for high-variance error detection, and maintained Dice = 0.929 ± 0.010 over 50-step rollout versus 0.423 for a deterministic U-Net.
infographic.watch_item: Inference is still relatively costly for a surrogate (~3.6 s per step), so deployment will need careful tradeoffs between uncertainty quality, rollout length, and runtime.
infographic.paper_title: Uncertainty-Aware Crack Growth Forecasting via Conditional Denoising Diffusion Models for Phase-Field Fracture
